stem: What is the least non-negative residue of \( [[a]]^{2} \) modulo \( [[n]] \)? (Equivalently, the remainder when \( [[a]]^{2} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).) This residue is a quadratic residue modulo \( [[n]] \).
solution: Square \( [[a]] \) and reduce modulo \( [[n]] \): the least non-negative residue of \( [[a]]^{2} \) is \( [[answer]] \), so \( [[answer]] \) is a quadratic residue modulo \( [[n]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[n]] \); the exponent 2 was dropped.
This is \( 2\cdot[[a]] \bmod [[n]] \); the square was replaced by doubling.
This is \( [[a]]^{2}+1 \bmod [[n]] \), an off-by-one slip.
This is one more than the correct residue.
This is two more than the correct residue.
This is \( [[a]]^{3} \bmod [[n]] \), a cube rather than a square.
This is three more than the correct residue.

stem: Every perfect square is congruent to 0 or 1 modulo 4, so a sum of two perfect squares can never be congruent to 3 modulo 4. To check whether \( [[n]] \) could possibly be written as a sum of two perfect squares, what is \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \)?
solution: Divide \( [[n]] \) by 4 and take the remainder: \( [[n]] \bmod 4 = [[answer]] \). If this were 3, \( [[n]] \) could be ruled out immediately as a sum of two squares.
distractor_rationales: This reduces modulo 2 instead of modulo 4, which only tests parity and cannot rule out residue 3.
This reduces modulo 3, the wrong modulus for this classification.
This is one more than the correct residue.
This is one less than the correct residue.
This reduces modulo 8, a finer classification than the one asked for.
This is the quotient of \( [[n]] \) by 4, not the remainder.

stem: For the odd prime \( [[p]] \), how many distinct nonzero quadratic residues (values of \( x^{2} \bmod [[p]] \) for \( x = 1, \dots, [[p]]-1 \)) are there modulo \( [[p]] \)?
solution: For an odd prime \( [[p]] \), squaring is exactly 2-to-1 on the nonzero residues \( \{1,\dots,[[p]]-1\} \) (since \( x^2 \equiv (-x)^2 \) and \( x \ne -x \)), so there are exactly \( \dfrac{[[p]]-1}{2} = [[answer]] \) distinct nonzero quadratic residues.
distractor_rationales: This counts every nonzero residue, forgetting that squaring is 2-to-1.
This counts every residue including 0.
This rounds the wrong way, using \( p+1 \) instead of \( p-1 \) in the numerator.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
This doubles the correct count, undoing the 2-to-1 reduction.

stem: By Euler's criterion, \( a \) is a quadratic residue modulo the odd prime \( p \) exactly when \( a^{(p-1)/2} \equiv 1 \pmod p \) (and a non-residue when it is \( \equiv -1 \)). What is the least non-negative residue of \( [[a]]^{([[p]]-1)/2} \) modulo \( [[p]] \)?
solution: Compute \( [[a]]^{([[p]]-1)/2} \bmod [[p]] \) directly: the result is \( [[answer]] \), which by Euler's criterion is either \( 1 \) (\( [[a]] \) is a quadratic residue mod \( [[p]] \)) or \( [[p]]-1 \) (\( [[a]] \) is a non-residue).
distractor_rationales: This assumes \( [[a]] \) is always a quadratic residue, guessing 1 without computing.
This assumes \( [[a]] \) is always a non-residue, guessing \( p-1 \) without computing.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[p]] \), as if the exponent were 1 instead of \( (p-1)/2 \).
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[p]] \), the wrong exponent.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is the complementary value \( p - \text{answer} \), the wrong sign class.

stem: For the odd prime \( [[p]] \), how many integers \( x \) with \( 0 \le x < [[p]] \) satisfy \( x^{2} \equiv [[a]] \pmod{[[p]]} \)? (This count is 2 if \( [[a]] \) is a nonzero quadratic residue modulo \( [[p]] \), and 0 if \( [[a]] \) is a non-residue.)
solution: By Euler's criterion, \( [[a]]^{([[p]]-1)/2} \equiv \pm 1 \pmod{[[p]]} \), and the number of solutions to \( x^{2} \equiv [[a]] \pmod{[[p]]} \) equals \( \left([[a]]^{([[p]]-1)/2}+1\right) \bmod [[p]] \), which is \( 2 \) when \( [[a]] \) is a quadratic residue and \( 0 \) otherwise. Here the count is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes every quadratic congruence has exactly one solution, the way a linear equation would.
This is the raw Euler's-criterion residue ( \( \pm 1 \bmod [[p]] \) ) without the final shift that converts it into a solution count.
This assumes \( [[a]] \) is always a quadratic residue, guessing 2 without checking.
This assumes \( [[a]] \) is always a non-residue, guessing 0 without checking.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is the complementary count \( p - \text{answer} \), the wrong direction.
This is just \( [[a]] \bmod [[p]] \), not the number of solutions.
